context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m stuck on Problem 8.1 rn, esp how to find the normal modes/frequencies for the 3×3 bead grid. idk how to set up the equations cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before tackling the 2D grid, think about what you already know: how did you handle normal modes for a 1D beaded string? What was the key trick that made the equations manageable?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting on Problem 8.1. I should use guided discovery to help them think about how to approach a 2D system by connecting it to what they likely already know: the 1D beaded string. I won't give any setup directly. A single targeted question will get them thinking in the right direction without over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: i think the trick was assuming a sinusoidal shape across the beads, like a discrete sine mode bc the ends are fixed? but i’m not totally sure how that turns into 2D.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right for 1D. Now, in your 2D grid you have two spatial directions: x and y. Do you think the mode shape in x and the mode shape in y can be treated independently of each other? What would that imply about the form of your solution?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the 1D approach. Now I should push them to think about how to extend it to 2D using separability, without giving it away. A targeted question about what happens when you have two independent spatial directions.

exchanges.student: maybe yeah, like separable? so maybe \(z_{jk}\) is something like \(X_k Y_j e^{i\omega t}\), and then \(X,Y\) are each sine-y bc fixed boundaries.
exchanges.tutor: Great intuition. So now write down the equation of motion for a single bead at position (j,k). What forces act on it, and from which neighboring beads?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the separable ansatz. Now I should push them to write down the actual discrete sine forms for X_k and Y_j, and then substitute into the equation of motion to find the frequency. I'll ask them to write the equation of motion for a single bead first, since that's the crucial next step.

exchanges.student: for bead \((j,k)\), i think the vertical displacement \(z_{jk}\) gets coupled to left/right neighbors by \(T_h\) and up/down by \(T_v\). so maybe \(m\ddot z_{jk}=T_h(z_{j,k+1}+z_{j,k-1}-2z_{jk})+T_v(z_{j+1,k}+z_{j-1,k}-2z_{jk})\)?
exchanges.tutor: That equation of motion is correct. Now substitute your separable ansatz into it. What do you get when you plug in and simplify?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the equation of motion. Now I should push them to substitute their separable ansatz into this equation and see what falls out for the frequency. This is the key algebraic step that connects the ansatz to the dispersion relation.

exchanges.student: i get \(-m\omega^2 X_kY_j = T_h(X_{k+1}+X_{k-1}-2X_k)Y_j + T_vX_k(Y_{j+1}+Y_{j-1}-2Y_j)\). then dividing by \(X_kY_j\) seems like it splits into an x-part plus a y-part.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — you've separated it cleanly. Now, for each part individually, what does the 1D beaded string result tell you about the value of terms like (X_{k+1}+X_{k-1}-2X_k)/X_k for a discrete sine mode with fixed boundaries? What are the allowed values?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly separated the equation. Now I should push them to identify what each part equals — specifically, to recall what the 1D result gives for the discrete sine modes with fixed boundary conditions, and apply it to get the explicit frequency formula.

exchanges.student: i think for fixed ends the allowed shapes are \(X_k\propto \sin\!\big(\frac{p\pi k}{4}\big)\) and \(Y_j\propto \sin\!\big(\frac{q\pi j}{4}\big)\) since there are 3 interior beads. then the discrete second-difference gives something like \(-4\sin^2(\frac{p\pi}{8})\) maybe?
exchanges.tutor: You have the right structure — good. But double-check that sin² argument carefully. What exactly do you get when you compute X_{k+1}+X_{k-1}-2X_k for X_k = sin(pπk/4), using the sum-to-product identity? Work it through step by step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right discrete sine modes and is on the right track with the second-difference formula, but the argument inside the sin² needs to be checked carefully. I should prompt them to verify this by working through the algebra explicitly rather than just accepting the formula.

exchanges.student: ok lemme try: \(X_{k+1}+X_{k-1}=2\sin(p\pi k/4)\cos(p\pi/4)\), so subtracting \(2X_k\) gives \(2X_k(\cos(p\pi/4)-1)\). so yeah that should be \(-4X_k\sin^2(p\pi/8)\), right?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct. So now you have both pieces. Can you put them together to write the full expression for ω² in terms of p, q, T_h, T_v, and m?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the second-difference result. Now I should push them to combine both x and y parts to write the full frequency formula for the normal modes.

exchanges.student:
yeah, i think  
\[
\omega_{pq}^2=\frac{4T_h}{m}\sin^2\!\left(\frac{p\pi}{8}\right)+\frac{4T_v}{m}\sin^2\!\left(\frac{q\pi}{8}\right),
\]
with \(p,q=1,2,3\). and the mode shape is \(z_{jk}\propto \sin\!\left(\frac{p\pi k}{4}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{q\pi j}{4}\right)e^{i\omega t}\).
exchanges.tutor: Excellent — that's part (a) done correctly. Now for part (b), with Tv = 1000·Th, think about what dominates ω²_{pq}. How does that help you rank the 9 modes by frequency?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the full dispersion relation and mode shapes. This completes part (a). I should affirm this and now push them toward part (b), where they need to order the 9 modes by frequency given Tv = 1000·Th and draw the displacement diagrams.

exchanges.student: since \(T_v\) is huge, the \(q\)-part dominates hard, so i should sort mostly by \(q\) first and only use \(p\) to break ties. i’m a little fuzzy on whether bigger \(q\) means bigger freq here, but i think yes.
exchanges.tutor: Right idea. To be sure, just compare sin²(π/8), sin²(2π/8), sin²(3π/8) — which is largest? Once you confirm the ordering in q, can you list all 9 (p,q) pairs in order of increasing frequency?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that q dominates and suspects larger q means higher frequency. I should confirm their reasoning direction and push them to actually compute or compare the sin² values for q=1,2,3 to be sure, then rank all 9 modes explicitly.

exchanges.tutor: That ordering is correct. Now for the diagrams: for each mode (p,q), the sign at position (j,k) is determined by sin(pπk/4)·sin(qπj/4). Try working out the signs for mode (1,1) first — what do you get at each of the 9 bead positions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly ordered the 9 modes. Now I should push them to the final step: drawing the displacement diagrams for each mode using the sine mode shapes. I'll ask them to work out the signs for a specific mode to get them started, rather than explaining the general procedure.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session throughout all 10 exchanges. The student was guided step-by-step from recalling 1D normal modes to deriving the full 2D dispersion relation and mode ordering, with the tutor never giving away answers but consistently prompting the student to do the reasoning. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready answers, never rewrote student work, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned through them. Every key step (separability, equation of motion, substitution, discrete second-difference, final frequency formula, mode ordering) was elicited from the student via targeted questions. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the separable ansatz, the correct EOM, the correct sin² result) and diagnosed confusion precisely before responding (e.g., asking the student to verify the sin² argument step-by-step when it was slightly off). 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level guidance, such as directing the student to use the sum-to-product identity and to work step-by-step, which is method-level feedback. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift occurred; the conversation advanced steadily through the problem. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, well-targeted, and supportive throughout, with no monologues or tone issues.
